Unlike conventional portals that charge listing fees, Find My Move operates on an advertising-supported model. The founders suggest this approach could prove particularly valuable for agents facing financial pressures in the current market. Vine commented: "Many agents felt trapped paying increasingly large sums simply to showcase their properties. We wanted to create a more accessible route to market."

The platform's early adoption figures appear to support this premise, with Moss noting: "The engagement levels we're seeing demonstrate a clear appetite for alternative property solutions." Industry observers might view this development as part of a broader trend towards diversifying property marketing channels.

For homeowners and landlords considering property portals, this new entrant could represent an additional avenue to explore when marketing a home. With the platform currently free to use and showing strong early growth, property professionals might wish to monitor its evolution in the coming months as the founders work to expand their user base and commercial partnerships.

item.title: Redundancy talks underway – but estate agency eyes ‘key hires’
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Knight Frank has announced new appointments to its affiliate broker programme whilst simultaneously entering redundancy consultations affecting parts of its UK workforce. The estate agency confirmed an undisclosed number of roles are under review, with industry sources suggesting between 90 and 100 employees could be impacted.

Affiliate broker expansion continues

The company is pressing ahead with its innovative affiliate model, which enables self-employed brokers to operate independently under the Knight Frank brand. These brokers handle client relationships and business development whilst receiving operational and marketing support from the firm. The structure also allows them to earn referral fees by introducing clients to Knight Frank's wider property services.

Three experienced agents - Simon Boulton, Mark Lee and Mark Ruffell - have been named as the first affiliate brokers. All three previously held leadership roles within Knight Frank's London operations. The company reports they've already completed several high-value transactions, including:

  • A £6.25m penthouse sale in Butler's Wharf
  • A £6m apartment transaction in Casson Square

London growth strategy

This initiative forms part of Knight Frank's ambitious plan to establish presence across 36 London locations. The firm has specifically identified Putney, Totteridge and Kingston as target markets for expansion. Rory Penn, Head of London Residential Sales at Knight Frank, commented: "This marks a pivotal moment for our London residential business. Our first affiliate brokers bring exceptional track records in prime London markets."

Industry observers note this dual approach of workforce restructuring alongside strategic hiring reflects the challenging conditions in the UK property market. The affiliate model potentially offers Knight Frank greater flexibility to scale operations up or down according to market demand.

As the redundancy consultation progresses, the company's focus appears firmly fixed on this new brokerage model as a key component of its future growth strategy in London's competitive property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Knight Frank has announced new appointments to its affiliate broker programme whilst simultaneously entering redundancy consultations affecting parts of its UK workforce. The estate agency confirmed an undisclosed number of roles are under review, with industry sources suggesting between 90 and 100 employees could be impacted.

Affiliate broker expansion continues

The company is pressing ahead with its innovative affiliate model, which enables self-employed brokers to operate independently under the Knight Frank brand. These brokers handle client relationships and business development whilst receiving operational and marketing support from the firm. The structure also allows them to earn referral fees by introducing clients to Knight Frank's wider property services.

Three experienced agents - Simon Boulton, Mark Lee and Mark Ruffell - have been named as the first affiliate brokers. All three previously held leadership roles within Knight Frank's London operations. The company reports they've already completed several high-value transactions, including:

  • A £6.25m penthouse sale in Butler's Wharf
  • A £6m apartment transaction in Casson Square

London growth strategy

This initiative forms part of Knight Frank's ambitious plan to establish presence across 36 London locations. The firm has specifically identified Putney, Totteridge and Kingston as target markets for expansion. Rory Penn, Head of London Residential Sales at Knight Frank, commented: "This marks a pivotal moment for our London residential business. Our first affiliate brokers bring exceptional track records in prime London markets."

Industry observers note this dual approach of workforce restructuring alongside strategic hiring reflects the challenging conditions in the UK property market. The affiliate model potentially offers Knight Frank greater flexibility to scale operations up or down according to market demand.

As the redundancy consultation progresses, the company's focus appears firmly fixed on this new brokerage model as a key component of its future growth strategy in London's competitive property landscape.

item.title: Greater education needed? Student landlords left confused by Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has brought significant changes to the UK’s rental market, but student landlords are reportedly struggling to navigate its implications. Many are turning to social media and online forums for guidance, highlighting a lack of clarity around how the legislation applies to student-specific tenancies. This confusion could lead to delays in regaining possession, void periods, and lost rental income, according to student lettings agency loc8me.

Challenges for Student Landlords

Unlike traditional private rentals, the student housing market operates on a unique academic cycle, with tenancies typically aligned to the academic year. Fern Tebbutt, director of operations at loc8me, notes that parts of the Renters’ Rights Act, while logical for standard private landlords, don’t necessarily address the specific challenges faced by student landlords. This has left many feeling excluded from the broader conversation around the legislation.

Areas of particular confusion include Ground 4A, a new possession ground designed for certain student houses in multiple occupation (HMOs). Some landlords mistakenly believe this applies to all student properties, when in fact one and two-bedroom homes are excluded. Errors in serving notice or preparing tenancy documentation could prevent landlords from relying on this ground when seeking possession ahead of the next academic year.

Misconceptions Around Section 21

Another common misunderstanding is the abolition of Section 21, which has led some landlords to believe they can no longer regain possession of their properties. Whilst the process has changed, loc8me emphasises that possession remains possible through statutory grounds, provided the correct procedures are followed. This includes adhering to notice periods and ensuring documentation is accurate.

There are also concerns that the reforms could lead to increased numbers of students ending tenancies mid-academic year. However, whilst tenants now have greater flexibility, they must still follow the legal process and provide the required notice to terminate a tenancy. This suggests that the student rental market’s cyclical nature is likely to remain intact.

Geographical Hotspots of Confusion

According to loc8me, self-managing landlords in cities with large student populations, such as Nottingham, Leeds, Manchester, Bristol, and Liverpool, are among the most affected by the uncertainty. These areas are also adapting to wider regulatory changes, adding to the complexity. The agency warns that relying on outdated guidance or informal online commentary could result in costly mistakes.

With demand for student accommodation continuing to outstrip supply in many university towns, it’s crucial for landlords to stay informed and seek professional advice where necessary. Clearer guidance tailored to the student rental market could help alleviate some of the confusion and ensure smoother management of student properties.

As the Renters’ Rights Act continues to reshape the rental landscape, student landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the specific provisions affecting their sector. Doing so could help avoid unnecessary complications and ensure compliance with the new regulations.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has brought significant changes to the UK’s rental market, but student landlords are reportedly struggling to navigate its implications. Many are turning to social media and online forums for guidance, highlighting a lack of clarity around how the legislation applies to student-specific tenancies. This confusion could lead to delays in regaining possession, void periods, and lost rental income, according to student lettings agency loc8me.

Challenges for Student Landlords

Unlike traditional private rentals, the student housing market operates on a unique academic cycle, with tenancies typically aligned to the academic year. Fern Tebbutt, director of operations at loc8me, notes that parts of the Renters’ Rights Act, while logical for standard private landlords, don’t necessarily address the specific challenges faced by student landlords. This has left many feeling excluded from the broader conversation around the legislation.

Areas of particular confusion include Ground 4A, a new possession ground designed for certain student houses in multiple occupation (HMOs). Some landlords mistakenly believe this applies to all student properties, when in fact one and two-bedroom homes are excluded. Errors in serving notice or preparing tenancy documentation could prevent landlords from relying on this ground when seeking possession ahead of the next academic year.

Misconceptions Around Section 21

Another common misunderstanding is the abolition of Section 21, which has led some landlords to believe they can no longer regain possession of their properties. Whilst the process has changed, loc8me emphasises that possession remains possible through statutory grounds, provided the correct procedures are followed. This includes adhering to notice periods and ensuring documentation is accurate.

There are also concerns that the reforms could lead to increased numbers of students ending tenancies mid-academic year. However, whilst tenants now have greater flexibility, they must still follow the legal process and provide the required notice to terminate a tenancy. This suggests that the student rental market’s cyclical nature is likely to remain intact.

Geographical Hotspots of Confusion

According to loc8me, self-managing landlords in cities with large student populations, such as Nottingham, Leeds, Manchester, Bristol, and Liverpool, are among the most affected by the uncertainty. These areas are also adapting to wider regulatory changes, adding to the complexity. The agency warns that relying on outdated guidance or informal online commentary could result in costly mistakes.

With demand for student accommodation continuing to outstrip supply in many university towns, it’s crucial for landlords to stay informed and seek professional advice where necessary. Clearer guidance tailored to the student rental market could help alleviate some of the confusion and ensure smoother management of student properties.

As the Renters’ Rights Act continues to reshape the rental landscape, student landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the specific provisions affecting their sector. Doing so could help avoid unnecessary complications and ensure compliance with the new regulations.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #442
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords across the UK are being urged to take note of recent changes to housing regulations, as fines for failing to address serious property hazards have risen significantly. With penalties now reaching up to £7,000, property owners may wish to ensure their rental homes meet all safety and maintenance standards to avoid costly consequences.

Increased Penalties for Property Hazards

The updated regulations target 21 specific hazards classified as ‘serious’, including issues such as damp, mould, and structural problems. Local authorities have been granted greater powers to enforce these rules, meaning landlords who fail to act on identified hazards could face substantial fines. This move aims to improve living conditions for tenants and ensure rental properties are safe and habitable.

Landlords might consider conducting regular property inspections to identify and address potential issues before they escalate. Proactive maintenance not only helps avoid penalties but also fosters positive relationships with tenants, reducing the risk of disputes or complaints.

Why These Changes Matter

The increase in fines reflects a broader push to raise standards in the private rental sector. With housing affordability and quality remaining key concerns, regulators are taking stronger action to hold landlords accountable for the condition of their properties. For tenants, these changes offer greater protection and reassurance that their homes will meet essential safety standards.

For landlords, the updated rules serve as a reminder of the importance of compliance. Whilst the financial impact of fines can be significant, there are also reputational risks to consider. Letting agents and property managers may wish to support landlords in navigating these changes, ensuring all legal obligations are met.

Steps Landlords Can Take

To stay on the right side of the law, landlords might consider the following steps:

  • Conduct regular property inspections to identify and address hazards promptly.
  • Ensure all repairs and maintenance work is carried out to a high standard.
  • Stay informed about changes to housing regulations and legal requirements.
  • Seek professional advice if unsure about compliance or necessary improvements.

Taking these proactive measures can help landlords avoid fines and maintain a positive reputation in the rental market. Additionally, ensuring properties are safe and well-maintained can attract and retain tenants, reducing vacancy periods and maximising rental income.

As the regulatory landscape continues to evolve, staying informed and proactive is essential for landlords. By prioritising property maintenance and compliance, landlords can protect their investments whilst providing safe and comfortable homes for their tenants.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords across the UK are being urged to take note of recent changes to housing regulations, as fines for failing to address serious property hazards have risen significantly. With penalties now reaching up to £7,000, property owners may wish to ensure their rental homes meet all safety and maintenance standards to avoid costly consequences.

Increased Penalties for Property Hazards

The updated regulations target 21 specific hazards classified as ‘serious’, including issues such as damp, mould, and structural problems. Local authorities have been granted greater powers to enforce these rules, meaning landlords who fail to act on identified hazards could face substantial fines. This move aims to improve living conditions for tenants and ensure rental properties are safe and habitable.

Landlords might consider conducting regular property inspections to identify and address potential issues before they escalate. Proactive maintenance not only helps avoid penalties but also fosters positive relationships with tenants, reducing the risk of disputes or complaints.

Why These Changes Matter

The increase in fines reflects a broader push to raise standards in the private rental sector. With housing affordability and quality remaining key concerns, regulators are taking stronger action to hold landlords accountable for the condition of their properties. For tenants, these changes offer greater protection and reassurance that their homes will meet essential safety standards.

For landlords, the updated rules serve as a reminder of the importance of compliance. Whilst the financial impact of fines can be significant, there are also reputational risks to consider. Letting agents and property managers may wish to support landlords in navigating these changes, ensuring all legal obligations are met.

Steps Landlords Can Take

To stay on the right side of the law, landlords might consider the following steps:

  • Conduct regular property inspections to identify and address hazards promptly.
  • Ensure all repairs and maintenance work is carried out to a high standard.
  • Stay informed about changes to housing regulations and legal requirements.
  • Seek professional advice if unsure about compliance or necessary improvements.

Taking these proactive measures can help landlords avoid fines and maintain a positive reputation in the rental market. Additionally, ensuring properties are safe and well-maintained can attract and retain tenants, reducing vacancy periods and maximising rental income.

As the regulatory landscape continues to evolve, staying informed and proactive is essential for landlords. By prioritising property maintenance and compliance, landlords can protect their investments whilst providing safe and comfortable homes for their tenants.

item.title: No region escapes as UK home sales fall sharply
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market has seen a noticeable slowdown in June 2026, with home sales falling across every region compared to the same period last year. During the first three weeks of the month, 75,000 properties were sold subject to contract – a 10.4% drop from June 2025's figures. While this suggests cooling market conditions, the broader context reveals a more nuanced picture when comparing recent years.

Regional variations in a cooling market

No part of the UK has escaped the recent dip in property transactions. Outer London experienced the most significant decline at 13.1%, followed closely by Inner London (12.7%) and the North West (11.5%). Scotland and Yorkshire and Humber proved relatively more resilient with decreases of 5.9% and 5.4% respectively, though still below their 2025 performance.

The regional breakdown shows:

  • East Midlands: 9.7% decrease
  • South East: 7.4% fall
  • Wales: 7.2% drop
  • West Midlands: 9.6% reduction

Longer-term market perspective

While the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the market remains stronger than in 2023 and pre-pandemic levels. Current sales are 6.5% higher than June 2023 and 8.4% above the 2017-19 average. The number of new property listings has shown resilience too, with year-to-date figures marginally ahead of 2025 and significantly above longer-term averages.

Key market indicators reveal:

  • Weekly average sales in 2026: 24,800 properties
  • Year-to-date listings: 895,000 (0.1% above 2025)
  • Current sales volumes remain 11.4% higher than 2023 levels

The data suggests the market is experiencing a correction from 2025's peak rather than a dramatic downturn. Homeowners considering selling might note that while conditions have softened, transaction levels remain healthy by historical standards. Those exploring mortgage options may find lenders increasingly competitive in this slightly cooler market environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market has seen a noticeable slowdown in June 2026, with home sales falling across every region compared to the same period last year. During the first three weeks of the month, 75,000 properties were sold subject to contract – a 10.4% drop from June 2025's figures. While this suggests cooling market conditions, the broader context reveals a more nuanced picture when comparing recent years.

Regional variations in a cooling market

No part of the UK has escaped the recent dip in property transactions. Outer London experienced the most significant decline at 13.1%, followed closely by Inner London (12.7%) and the North West (11.5%). Scotland and Yorkshire and Humber proved relatively more resilient with decreases of 5.9% and 5.4% respectively, though still below their 2025 performance.

The regional breakdown shows:

  • East Midlands: 9.7% decrease
  • South East: 7.4% fall
  • Wales: 7.2% drop
  • West Midlands: 9.6% reduction

Longer-term market perspective

While the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the market remains stronger than in 2023 and pre-pandemic levels. Current sales are 6.5% higher than June 2023 and 8.4% above the 2017-19 average. The number of new property listings has shown resilience too, with year-to-date figures marginally ahead of 2025 and significantly above longer-term averages.

Key market indicators reveal:

  • Weekly average sales in 2026: 24,800 properties
  • Year-to-date listings: 895,000 (0.1% above 2025)
  • Current sales volumes remain 11.4% higher than 2023 levels

The data suggests the market is experiencing a correction from 2025's peak rather than a dramatic downturn. Homeowners considering selling might note that while conditions have softened, transaction levels remain healthy by historical standards. Those exploring mortgage options may find lenders increasingly competitive in this slightly cooler market environment.

item.title: OPINION: Burnham’s Blairite house price bounce?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

As the political landscape shifts following the recent Downing Street departure, attention turns to the potential economic implications for the UK property market. With inflation pressures, rising public debt and economic uncertainty lingering, homeowners and prospective buyers may be wondering how a new premiership could affect housing policies and mortgage affordability.

Housing market resilience amidst economic challenges

Despite broader economic headwinds, the UK property market has shown notable resilience in recent months. Transaction levels and house prices have remained relatively stable across most regions, with the exception of prime central London markets. This stability comes despite:

  • Ongoing cost-of-living pressures
  • Higher interest rates compared to historic lows
  • Economic uncertainty affecting buyer confidence

Potential policy directions under new leadership

The incoming administration's approach to housing could significantly influence market dynamics in coming years. Early indications suggest several possible policy priorities:

  • Increased focus on social and council house building programmes
  • Potential reforms to planning regulations to boost construction
  • Continued support for first-time buyer schemes
  • Investment in regional infrastructure projects

Such measures could stimulate activity in the construction sector while potentially easing pressure on the private housing market. However, the precise impact on property prices and mortgage availability would depend on how these policies are implemented and funded.

Market reactions and economic considerations

Financial markets appear to be adopting a wait-and-see approach to the new political era. Recent indicators show:

  • Stable gilt yields suggesting cautious optimism
  • The FTSE 250 maintaining steady performance
  • Pound sterling showing resilience against major currencies

Mortgage borrowers might watch for any changes to fiscal policy that could influence interest rate decisions by the Bank of England. The commitment to existing fiscal rules may provide some reassurance, though the long-term economic strategy remains to be seen.

As the new government establishes its agenda, homeowners and buyers may wish to stay informed about potential policy changes that could affect housing affordability and mortgage products. Professional financial advice could help individuals navigate this period of transition.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

As the political landscape shifts following the recent Downing Street departure, attention turns to the potential economic implications for the UK property market. With inflation pressures, rising public debt and economic uncertainty lingering, homeowners and prospective buyers may be wondering how a new premiership could affect housing policies and mortgage affordability.

Housing market resilience amidst economic challenges

Despite broader economic headwinds, the UK property market has shown notable resilience in recent months. Transaction levels and house prices have remained relatively stable across most regions, with the exception of prime central London markets. This stability comes despite:

  • Ongoing cost-of-living pressures
  • Higher interest rates compared to historic lows
  • Economic uncertainty affecting buyer confidence

Potential policy directions under new leadership

The incoming administration's approach to housing could significantly influence market dynamics in coming years. Early indications suggest several possible policy priorities:

  • Increased focus on social and council house building programmes
  • Potential reforms to planning regulations to boost construction
  • Continued support for first-time buyer schemes
  • Investment in regional infrastructure projects

Such measures could stimulate activity in the construction sector while potentially easing pressure on the private housing market. However, the precise impact on property prices and mortgage availability would depend on how these policies are implemented and funded.

Market reactions and economic considerations

Financial markets appear to be adopting a wait-and-see approach to the new political era. Recent indicators show:

  • Stable gilt yields suggesting cautious optimism
  • The FTSE 250 maintaining steady performance
  • Pound sterling showing resilience against major currencies

Mortgage borrowers might watch for any changes to fiscal policy that could influence interest rate decisions by the Bank of England. The commitment to existing fiscal rules may provide some reassurance, though the long-term economic strategy remains to be seen.

As the new government establishes its agenda, homeowners and buyers may wish to stay informed about potential policy changes that could affect housing affordability and mortgage products. Professional financial advice could help individuals navigate this period of transition.

item.title: Are you selling property on one of the UK’s most Instagrammable streets?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Social media is reshaping how properties are marketed, with picturesque streets gaining attention through viral posts on platforms like Instagram and TikTok. New research from Lloyds Bank highlights the UK’s most photographed streets, revealing how online popularity can influence buyer interest. Whilst eye-catching aesthetics may draw attention, industry experts note that practical considerations—such as affordability, local amenities, and transport links—remain key factors in purchasing decisions.

How social media influences property appeal

Estate agents are increasingly using social platforms to showcase neighbourhood charm, from historic architecture to vibrant local culture. Viral posts can amplify an area’s desirability, but buyers typically weigh this against long-term needs. For example, Bath’s Royal Crescent—ranked the UK’s most Instagrammable street—boasts Georgian grandeur, yet its average house price of £590,786 may place it out of reach for many. Similarly, London’s Portobello Road, famed for its colourful facades and market buzz, commands an average price of £1.66m.

Property professionals suggest that whilst social media can spark initial interest, thorough research into an area’s practicality is essential. Factors like school catchments, commute times, and future development plans often carry more weight than a street’s photogenic qualities alone.

The UK’s top Instagrammable streets

Lloyds’ research identifies 20 highly shared locations across the country, blending historic charm with modern appeal. Notable entries include:

  • The Street, Castle Combe (Wiltshire) – Quintessential Cotswolds beauty (£428,759 average price)
  • Victoria Street, Edinburgh – A winding, colourful Old Town landmark (£414,596)
  • Anita Street, Manchester – A restored Victorian terrace in Ancoats (£257,319)
  • Windsor Esplanade, Cardiff Bay – Waterside views at a relatively accessible £198,125

However, the surge in online attention has drawbacks. Nigel Bishop of Recoco Property Search notes that some villages face disruptions from influencers seeking the perfect shot, potentially altering the tranquillity that originally made these locations desirable.

For homeowners considering selling, highlighting a property’s surroundings through social media could broaden its appeal—but balancing online allure with tangible benefits remains crucial for serious buyers.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Social media is reshaping how properties are marketed, with picturesque streets gaining attention through viral posts on platforms like Instagram and TikTok. New research from Lloyds Bank highlights the UK’s most photographed streets, revealing how online popularity can influence buyer interest. Whilst eye-catching aesthetics may draw attention, industry experts note that practical considerations—such as affordability, local amenities, and transport links—remain key factors in purchasing decisions.

How social media influences property appeal

Estate agents are increasingly using social platforms to showcase neighbourhood charm, from historic architecture to vibrant local culture. Viral posts can amplify an area’s desirability, but buyers typically weigh this against long-term needs. For example, Bath’s Royal Crescent—ranked the UK’s most Instagrammable street—boasts Georgian grandeur, yet its average house price of £590,786 may place it out of reach for many. Similarly, London’s Portobello Road, famed for its colourful facades and market buzz, commands an average price of £1.66m.

Property professionals suggest that whilst social media can spark initial interest, thorough research into an area’s practicality is essential. Factors like school catchments, commute times, and future development plans often carry more weight than a street’s photogenic qualities alone.

The UK’s top Instagrammable streets

Lloyds’ research identifies 20 highly shared locations across the country, blending historic charm with modern appeal. Notable entries include:

  • The Street, Castle Combe (Wiltshire) – Quintessential Cotswolds beauty (£428,759 average price)
  • Victoria Street, Edinburgh – A winding, colourful Old Town landmark (£414,596)
  • Anita Street, Manchester – A restored Victorian terrace in Ancoats (£257,319)
  • Windsor Esplanade, Cardiff Bay – Waterside views at a relatively accessible £198,125

However, the surge in online attention has drawbacks. Nigel Bishop of Recoco Property Search notes that some villages face disruptions from influencers seeking the perfect shot, potentially altering the tranquillity that originally made these locations desirable.

For homeowners considering selling, highlighting a property’s surroundings through social media could broaden its appeal—but balancing online allure with tangible benefits remains crucial for serious buyers.

item.title: It’s a knockout! Agents raise £15,000 for charity
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK property sector showed its charitable side earlier this month as industry professionals traded spreadsheets for boxing gloves at the annual Rumble with the Agents event. The white-collar boxing night, held in London on 11th June, raised an impressive £15,000 for North London Hospice through ticket sales, auctions and donations.

A Knockout Success

Now in its ninth year, the sold-out event brought together estate agents, landlords, developers and property suppliers for an evening of fundraising and networking. Founded by Landlord Action's Paul Shamplina, the initiative combines his passion for boxing with supporting worthy causes. This year's attendees enjoyed a full fight card alongside a three-course dinner, raffle and silent auction.

The event initially raised £11,665 through traditional fundraising activities, with additional contributions from the silent auction and the organising team pushing the final total to £15,000. Since its 2015 debut, Rumble with the Agents has generated over £200,000 for charities focused on homelessness, mental health and end-of-life care.

Industry Comes Together

Shamplina expressed his gratitude for the property sector's ongoing generosity: "North London Hospice provides vital support during life's most challenging moments. To raise £15,000 demonstrates our industry's commitment to giving back beyond business transactions." The event received sponsorship from mydeposits, with many attendees purchasing raffle tickets and bidding on auction items.

Pippa Baker from North London Hospice added: "This incredible donation will help ensure patients facing life-limiting illnesses receive compassionate end-of-life care. Community support through events like this has never been more crucial."

For those in the property sector interested in charitable initiatives, industry fundraising events can offer networking opportunities whilst supporting worthwhile causes. The success of Rumble with the Agents highlights how professional communities can make a tangible difference to local charities.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK property sector showed its charitable side earlier this month as industry professionals traded spreadsheets for boxing gloves at the annual Rumble with the Agents event. The white-collar boxing night, held in London on 11th June, raised an impressive £15,000 for North London Hospice through ticket sales, auctions and donations.

A Knockout Success

Now in its ninth year, the sold-out event brought together estate agents, landlords, developers and property suppliers for an evening of fundraising and networking. Founded by Landlord Action's Paul Shamplina, the initiative combines his passion for boxing with supporting worthy causes. This year's attendees enjoyed a full fight card alongside a three-course dinner, raffle and silent auction.

The event initially raised £11,665 through traditional fundraising activities, with additional contributions from the silent auction and the organising team pushing the final total to £15,000. Since its 2015 debut, Rumble with the Agents has generated over £200,000 for charities focused on homelessness, mental health and end-of-life care.

Industry Comes Together

Shamplina expressed his gratitude for the property sector's ongoing generosity: "North London Hospice provides vital support during life's most challenging moments. To raise £15,000 demonstrates our industry's commitment to giving back beyond business transactions." The event received sponsorship from mydeposits, with many attendees purchasing raffle tickets and bidding on auction items.

Pippa Baker from North London Hospice added: "This incredible donation will help ensure patients facing life-limiting illnesses receive compassionate end-of-life care. Community support through events like this has never been more crucial."

For those in the property sector interested in charitable initiatives, industry fundraising events can offer networking opportunities whilst supporting worthwhile causes. The success of Rumble with the Agents highlights how professional communities can make a tangible difference to local charities.

item.title: Before you test a Renters’ Rights Act loophole…
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Bill represents one of the most significant changes to private renting in England for decades. As with any major legislative shift, some landlords may be tempted to explore potential loopholes in the new rules. However, legal experts warn that testing untested provisions could prove risky until courts provide definitive interpretations.

Why caution is advised with new rental legislation

When substantial new housing laws come into force, grey areas inevitably emerge around specific provisions. The Renters' Reform Bill introduces numerous changes affecting deposits, evictions and tenancy terms - some of which may not be fully clarified until challenged in court. Property professionals suggest landlords avoid attempting creative interpretations of the rules before proper legal precedents are established.

One area attracting particular attention involves restrictions on rent collection. The legislation prohibits landlords from taking any rent before signing a tenancy agreement, limiting advance payments to just one month's rent between signing and the tenancy start date. Some have speculated about making tenancies conditional upon this payment, but government sources indicate such clauses may not hold up legally.

Potential risks of conditional tenancy clauses

Consider a hypothetical scenario where a landlord includes a clause making the tenancy conditional upon receiving the first month's rent upfront. If the tenant fails to pay and the landlord withdraws the property, they could potentially face legal action. The tenant might claim compensation for any additional rent paid elsewhere if they can prove the conditional clause was invalid under the new legislation.

Until courts rule on such cases, the legality of various approaches remains uncertain. Landlords might consider:

  • Seeking professional legal advice before implementing any novel tenancy terms
  • Following the legislation's clear provisions rather than speculative interpretations
  • Monitoring official guidance from the new Ombudsman service once established

While the desire to protect rental income is understandable, attempting untested approaches could lead to costly disputes. As the rental market adapts to these reforms, patience and compliance may prove wiser than rushing to test legislative boundaries.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Reform Bill represents one of the most significant changes to private renting in England for decades. As with any major legislative shift, some landlords may be tempted to explore potential loopholes in the new rules. However, legal experts warn that testing untested provisions could prove risky until courts provide definitive interpretations.

Why caution is advised with new rental legislation

When substantial new housing laws come into force, grey areas inevitably emerge around specific provisions. The Renters' Reform Bill introduces numerous changes affecting deposits, evictions and tenancy terms - some of which may not be fully clarified until challenged in court. Property professionals suggest landlords avoid attempting creative interpretations of the rules before proper legal precedents are established.

One area attracting particular attention involves restrictions on rent collection. The legislation prohibits landlords from taking any rent before signing a tenancy agreement, limiting advance payments to just one month's rent between signing and the tenancy start date. Some have speculated about making tenancies conditional upon this payment, but government sources indicate such clauses may not hold up legally.

Potential risks of conditional tenancy clauses

Consider a hypothetical scenario where a landlord includes a clause making the tenancy conditional upon receiving the first month's rent upfront. If the tenant fails to pay and the landlord withdraws the property, they could potentially face legal action. The tenant might claim compensation for any additional rent paid elsewhere if they can prove the conditional clause was invalid under the new legislation.

Until courts rule on such cases, the legality of various approaches remains uncertain. Landlords might consider:

  • Seeking professional legal advice before implementing any novel tenancy terms
  • Following the legislation's clear provisions rather than speculative interpretations
  • Monitoring official guidance from the new Ombudsman service once established

While the desire to protect rental income is understandable, attempting untested approaches could lead to costly disputes. As the rental market adapts to these reforms, patience and compliance may prove wiser than rushing to test legislative boundaries.

item.title: The new viewing question: how hot does this property get?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Heat resilience becomes key consideration for UK homebuyers

As Britain experiences more frequent heatwaves, property hunters are increasingly factoring summer comfort into their purchasing decisions. Estate agents report growing enquiries about how homes perform during extreme temperatures, marking a shift from the traditional focus solely on winter warmth. With the UK recording its highest ever temperature of 40.3°C in 2022, features like effective ventilation, shading solutions and thermal efficiency are moving up buyers' checklists.

Changing priorities in property features

Whilst energy-efficient heating systems and double glazing remain important, homeowners might now consider:

  • South-facing rooms that don't overheat in summer months
  • Bedrooms that maintain comfortable temperatures during heatwaves
  • Home offices with adequate ventilation for year-round use
  • Properties with smart shading solutions or thermal mass materials

Estate agents note that modern, well-insulated homes designed for energy efficiency can sometimes struggle most with heat retention during prolonged warm spells. This has led some buyers to favour period properties with thicker walls or traditional design features that naturally regulate temperature.

The emerging role of cooling systems

Though still relatively uncommon in UK homes, cooling systems are beginning to attract buyer attention, particularly in certain market segments. Luxury properties and new-build apartments might find air conditioning becomes a differentiating feature rather than an unusual extra. For those viewing multiple properties during hot weather, stepping into a comfortably cooled space could create a memorable positive impression.

Property professionals suggest that while cooling systems may not currently add significant value to most homes, this could change if extreme summer temperatures become the norm. The evolving climate means UK homeowners may wish to consider both winter warmth and summer comfort when making property decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Heat resilience becomes key consideration for UK homebuyers

As Britain experiences more frequent heatwaves, property hunters are increasingly factoring summer comfort into their purchasing decisions. Estate agents report growing enquiries about how homes perform during extreme temperatures, marking a shift from the traditional focus solely on winter warmth. With the UK recording its highest ever temperature of 40.3°C in 2022, features like effective ventilation, shading solutions and thermal efficiency are moving up buyers' checklists.

Changing priorities in property features

Whilst energy-efficient heating systems and double glazing remain important, homeowners might now consider:

  • South-facing rooms that don't overheat in summer months
  • Bedrooms that maintain comfortable temperatures during heatwaves
  • Home offices with adequate ventilation for year-round use
  • Properties with smart shading solutions or thermal mass materials

Estate agents note that modern, well-insulated homes designed for energy efficiency can sometimes struggle most with heat retention during prolonged warm spells. This has led some buyers to favour period properties with thicker walls or traditional design features that naturally regulate temperature.

The emerging role of cooling systems

Though still relatively uncommon in UK homes, cooling systems are beginning to attract buyer attention, particularly in certain market segments. Luxury properties and new-build apartments might find air conditioning becomes a differentiating feature rather than an unusual extra. For those viewing multiple properties during hot weather, stepping into a comfortably cooled space could create a memorable positive impression.

Property professionals suggest that while cooling systems may not currently add significant value to most homes, this could change if extreme summer temperatures become the norm. The evolving climate means UK homeowners may wish to consider both winter warmth and summer comfort when making property decisions.

item.title: Where have all the rental homes gone?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~
item.content:encoded: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~
